narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 41.6% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 30, Year 1 and Year 3 deciles both at 3, middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is much higher at 37.27, and typical Year 3 deciles reach 5.91, highlighting how your profile stacks up against those who succeeded. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your undergraduate GPA, family income, and Year 3 decile, in that order of impact. Interestingly, your GPA actually worked in your favor, nudging the model toward predicting success since it's close to the 3.24 average of bar passers. Your middle family income also helped, as it's common among successful students—35.3% of them share this bracket—pulling the prediction away from failure. However, your Year 3 decile of 3 had the strongest negative pull among these, driving the model toward expecting a bar failure because it lags behind the typical 5.91 for those who passed. Your lower LSAT and Year 1 decile contributed less directly but still signaled risks compared to admitted peers. Full-time status aligns well with 93.2% of bar passers, so it didn't hurt you there. Overall, while some strengths like your GPA and background softened the outlook, the gaps in standardized testing and later-year performance tipped the scales against admission. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your grades in advanced coursework to improve your decile ranking, and gathering letters from mentors who can speak to your potential. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these areas and reapplying—your solid GPA shows you have a strong foundation to build on. With targeted preparation, you can shift the odds in your favor for the next cycle.
